author: @scloh1
text: I don't think you need both sides of the rocker to recieve water. One side can just be a fixed weight that is less than a full (of water) opposing side where the water goes. When the water side empties, the fixed weight should supply the return force. I think that would make the design and any motion and calculations simpler and more consistent, although it might lose some symmetry if that forms part of the aesthetic. I would like to see a compact one of these where the return force is supplies by a spring or elastic band. No reason, just I think it would look funny. Love the entire series of these videos BTW.

author: @daveapplemotors
text:
You can get constant water pressure if you keep a constant water column height. Simple physics. 
A natural pressure regulator and use an overflow hose with return to reservoir.  It doesn't matter how you overfill your column  or how you power it.

author: @HelgeMoulding
text: You control the flow rate by the size of the opening on the bottom of the funnel. Then you just have a reservoir at the top. Water clocks are thousands of years old. This just adds gears to a device that the Egyptian pharaos used.

replies.text: what device was that, any links so I can prepare for the apocalypse? :p No but seriously do you have a link? I'm very curious as to what it looks like. text:
Hey Rob, are these small enough to print from the first Elegoo that you reviewed?  I've been having troubles with some of your designs(like the Weird Water Motor), because the printer is too small to print them. 
Keep up the good work.

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: I'm not Rob , but you can scale it down (objects in the printing files) , to fit your printer capabilities. Of course , in case of water motor (this one , from the video) , sequence of the motion (or the frequency) , of the prime mover would be different , with smaller part(s) , depending on a flow (volume/mass) of water. Or you can cut the parts in half , or more parts and glue them together afterwards .
